title: People & Process
subtitle: The most common way things break

hook.metaphor: Ask any head chef where the night goes wrong and they won't say 'the oven' — they'll say 'people and the system we work to.' The new waiter who rings up the wrong table. The missing checklist that lets a raw dish leave the pass. Most operational losses aren't dramatic disasters; they're small human slips and sloppy processes, repeated.
hook.scenario: A junior trader fat-fingers an extra zero and buys 10× what was intended. Was this a 'bad person' problem — or did the process let one tired human cause a catastrophe with no safety net?

learn: Two of the four sources — **people** and **process** — cause the lion's share of everyday operational losses. They're deeply intertwined: a good process protects against human error; a bad process amplifies it.

**People risk** has an honest half and a dishonest half. The honest half is plain **human error** — fatigue, misunderstanding, a slip of the finger (our fat-finger trade). The dishonest half is **internal fraud** — someone deliberately abusing access for personal gain. Both are 'people', but you manage them differently: training and ergonomics for error, controls and culture for fraud.

**Process risk** — often called **execution risk** — is when the *way work flows* is flawed: a missing approval step, an unclear handoff, no reconciliation, a manual workaround that everyone relies on. The fat-finger trade is really a process failure: why was one person able to execute a giant trade with no second check or limit?

The most important mindset shift: **blaming the individual is usually the wrong fix.** If one tired person can cause a disaster, the *system* is the problem. Strong operational risk management designs processes that assume people will occasionally err — and catches the error before it becomes a loss. (This is also why 'tone at the top' from the governance level matters: culture decides whether people hide mistakes or surface them.)
bridge: So at work, when something goes wrong you can resist the urge to find someone to blame and instead ask: *what in the process let a normal human mistake turn into a loss?* The durable fix is almost always a better-designed process — a check, a limit, a second pair of eyes — not just telling people to 'be more careful.'

interactions.title: Whose fault — or what's the fix?
interactions.prompt: After the fat-finger trade, leadership debates what to do. Which response actually reduces future risk?

options.label: Fire the trader and move on.
options.feedback: Satisfying, but it fixes nothing. The next tired human will make the same slip. You've removed a person, not the weakness.

options.label: Add a confirmation step and size limits for large trades.
options.feedback: Yes — this is process design. You assume humans will err and build a net that catches it. The fix outlives any individual.

options.label: Send a company-wide email saying 'be careful'.
options.feedback: Awareness alone doesn't stop fatigue-driven slips. Hope is not a control. You need a structural check, not a reminder.

interactions.title: People or process?
interactions.prompt: Sort each issue — and notice how often they overlap.

items.label: A staff member deliberately approves fake invoices
There's no reconciliation between two systems
A tired clerk transposes digits on a payment
Large payments need no second approver
A handoff between teams has no clear owner

interactions.title: Key terms
interactions.prompt: Match each people/process term to its meaning.

pairs.right: An honest, unintentional slip or mistake
Deliberate abuse of access for personal gain
Loss from a flawed way of carrying out work
Cross-checking two records to catch discrepancies
Splitting a task so no one person controls it end-to-end

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Most operational losses come from ordinary human slips and weak processes — so the real fix is a better-designed process that catches mistakes, not just blaming the person.”

checkpoint.q: A 'fat-finger' trade (a typo causing a huge order) is best seen as…
options: A process failure that let a normal human error cause a loss
checkpoint.explain: If one slip can cause disaster, the process lacked a safety net — limits and confirmations are the fix.

options: Splitting a task so no single person controls it end-to-end
checkpoint.explain: Separating who initiates, approves, and records makes fraud and error far harder — a core process control.

checkpoint.q: The most durable response to a recurring human error is usually to…
options: Tell people to be more careful
Redesign the process to catch the error automatically
checkpoint.explain: Design for the fact that humans err; build a structural check rather than relying on vigilance.
